title: Western PA Trails — Deep Intel Database v5
sources_note: Compiled from trail councils, TrailLink/AllTrails reviews, PA DCNR, PA Fish & Boat Commission, PA Game Commission, local newspapers, city of Pittsburgh statements, Wikipedia, Heinz History Center, NPS, Fallingwater, Kentuck Knob, Rivers of Steel, 9/11 National Memorial Trail Alliance, wilderness outfitter websites, shuttle operator pages, cycling blogs, and community forums. All facts paraphrased. Verify current conditions, reservations, hours, and closures before visiting.

new_in_v5: Native American history: Nemacolin's Path (the 10,000-year corridor under the GAP), Queen Aliquippa (Mingo Seneca leader at McKeesport/Mon-Yough confluence, died 1754, visited by Washington 1753), Shannopin's Town (Delaware settlement in Lawrenceville), Ohiiyo etymology (origin of 'Ohio').

September 11 National Memorial Trail: 1,500-mile congressionally designated trail connecting Flight 93 / Pentagon / NYC memorials; GAP + C&O form ~1/3 of route; 1.5-mile purpose-built segment from Garrett trailhead opened April 2021; CSX donated 130 acres for Garrett-to-Berlin connector; 750 PA wayfinding signs being installed before Sept 11, 2026.
Flight 93 National Memorial: free, NPS-operated, Tower of Voices, 40 maple groves for 40 victims.
Ohiopyle whitewater: Middle Yough (Class I-II, 9-11 miles, min age 4-5, no permit) and Lower Yough (Class III-IV, 7.5 miles, min age 10-12, permit required weekends, dam-controlled year-round).
E-bike rules: PA single definition (≤750W, ≤100 lbs, ≤20 mph, pedals required); allowed on GAP/Montour; throttle-only prohibited on non-motorized trails; min age 16; not allowed on State Game Lands.
Family-friendly sections: Pittsburgh South Side to Point (4 mi paved); Ohiopyle State Park GAP (ADA); Montour Miles 20-30 (paved, Greer Tunnel, Tandem Connection).
Raccoon Creek State Park Wildflower Reserve: 700+ species, peak late April and August, NO DOGS in reserve, 525 Lincoln Hwy Clinton PA, ~30 min from Pittsburgh.
Meadowcroft Rockshelter: oldest human habitation site in North America (16,000-19,000 yr), 401 Meadowcroft Rd Avella PA, May-Nov 10am-4pm, $15 adults, call 724-587-3412.

Frank Lloyd Wright sites: Fallingwater (UNESCO, $30-80 per tour, advance booking required) and Kentuck Knob (7 miles from Fallingwater, open Mar-Dec); Wilderness Voyageurs shuttle from Ohiopyle $65 roundtrip for up to 4 people.
Camping detail: Kentuck Campground (200+ sites, electric, cottages, yurts, reservation required 888-PA-PARKS up to 11 months ahead); free hiker-biker sites at Miles 89/99/110/122.

Steel Valley industrial heritage: Carrie Furnace (1907-1978, NPS landmark, Rivers of Steel tours Sat-Sun May-Oct); Kennywood roller coasters visible from GAP; Carnegie coal-gas pipeline columns (90 pedestals near Mile 142); osprey nest at Port Perry; Mon Valley Works still active across river.
Elevation profile data added to GAP trail metadata and as standalone POI.
Airport connectivity: PIT Airport Connector → Montour Trail Mile 7.8 (bike-from-airport possible).
Dog rules formalized; water sources on Montour confirmed seasonal at 25+ trailheads.

maps_url uses coordinate-based Google Maps links (https://maps.google.com/?q=lat,lon) for all POIs with coordinates.
Named-place Google Maps search links used where coordinates unavailable.

Reservation/booking URLs added for: Kentuck Campground, Fallingwater, Kentuck Knob, Sager Mosaics, Western Maryland Scenic Railroad, Wilderness Voyageurs shuttles, Wheelzup Adventures, Amtrak, Laurel Highlands Trail shelters, Meadowcroft.
Trail authority pages linked for: gaptrail.org, montourtrail.org, panhandletrail.org, friendsoftheriverfront.org, rachelcarsontrails.org.
new_in_v5.2: FACT-CHECK PASS: Key facts verified via targeted searches June 2026.

CORRECTIONS: (1) National Tunnel: database now reflects $2.75M repair cost and 2027 earliest reopening (not 'unknown'). (2) Grist House Millvale: website URL corrected from 'grissthouse.com' to 'gristhouse.com'. (3) Connellsville Urgent Care: updated to reflect February 2026 opening; confirmed serves patients 3+ months old. (4) Sager Mosaics: tour price confirmed $30/person; Tues/Wed closure confirmed.
CONFIDENCE FIELD added to all 228 POIs (0-100 scale). See confidence_note for rationale.
verify_before_visit field added: True for small businesses not independently confirmed, and for anything with confidence < 65.
Score bands: 95-100=permanent/verified institution; 88-94=recently confirmed; 78-87=reliable secondary sources; 65-77=not independently checked; <65=flagged as uncertain.
integration_notes.coord_audit_2026_06: 7 POIs corrected (National Tunnel coords+twp, Greer Tunnel coords+mile, 3 Tandem Connection entries coords, McDonald mile, Queen Aliquippa trail) and 1 flagged (Raccoon Creek off-trail). Verified against Wikipedia, MTC, TrailLink, and trail mile markers.

montour.name: Montour Trail
montour.surface: crushed limestone; paved in Peters Township and Clairton; some on-road gaps near Clairton end
montour.grade: under 1% (slight variation crossing three watershed divides)
montour.region: south & west suburbs of Pittsburgh; Allegheny + Washington Counties
connects: gap (via Clairton Connector, partially on road)
three_rivers_heritage (via T light rail from Bethel Park end)

branches.bethel: 2.8 mi into Bethel Park; highest elevation on the trail; forested
branches.westland: 4.1 mi rural farmland spur beside an active tank-train rail line; open views
branches.airport: ~6 mi on-road/trail link from Enlow to PIT terminal

montour.named_after: Montour Run creek (followed for first 8.5 mi from Coraopolis end)
montour.history: Former Montour Railroad (1877–1985), built to haul coal from Imperial to the Ohio River. First trail section opened 1992 by all-volunteer Montour Trail Council.
montour.note: National Recreation Trail since 2017. 400,000+ users/year. More water stops than almost any comparable long trail.

seasonal_notes.spring: Wildflowers along Montour Run corridor (March-May). Verify National Tunnel and Greer Tunnel status before riding — both have been closed for work recently.
seasonal_notes.summer: Hot and often humid; plenty of shade in the northern creek corridor. Mountain laurel on Bethel Branch late June.
seasonal_notes.fall: October fall color: Peters Township / Greer Tunnel area especially colorful late Oct. Deer active at dusk.
seasonal_notes.winter: Not plowed but open for fat-tire bikes and cross-country skis. Tandem Connection rents both.

gap.name: Great Allegheny Passage (GAP)
gap.surface: crushed limestone (most); paved Pittsburgh to McKeesport
gap.grade: gentle railroad grade; long climb from Cumberland (~625 ft) to Eastern Continental Divide (~2,392 ft); downhill to Pittsburgh
gap.endpoints: Pittsburgh (Point State Park) to Cumberland, MD
gap.region: SW PA through Laurel Highlands into western MD
gap.history: Built on former Western Maryland Railway and P&LE Railroad. Completed 2013. Trail completed over 27 years by 7 volunteer trail organizations of the Allegheny Trail Alliance.
gap.note: Connects to C&O Canal Towpath at Cumberland for 335-mi Pittsburgh-to-DC route. Big Savage Tunnel closed Dec–early April. Mile markers in database are approximate from Pittsburgh end (mile 0).

seasonal_notes.spring: Peak wildflowers Ohiopyle: mid-April to mid-May. Trout season opens first Saturday of April. Big Savage Tunnel reopens early April.
seasonal_notes.summer: Ohiopyle is packed May-Sep. Yough river stays cool for fishing all summer. Book Ohiopyle lodging and outfitters far in advance.
seasonal_notes.fall: Best fall color: early Oct at the Divide/Meyersdale, mid-Oct at Ohiopyle, late Oct near Pittsburgh. Fishing prime for large brown trout.
seasonal_notes.winter: Big Savage Tunnel closed Dec-early April. Paved sections (Pittsburgh-McKeesport) rideable year-round. Trail is never plowed.

elevation_profile.note: Crushed limestone rail grade — net uphill Pittsburgh to Cumberland, rewarding downhill finish from Divide

panhandle.name: Panhandle Trail
panhandle.surface: paved asphalt through Washington County (~18 mi east of McDonald); crushed limestone in WV and east of McDonald
panhandle.grade: ~1%; steady climb to Bulger (high point), then downhill each way from there
panhandle.endpoints: Walkers Mill (Collier Twp, PA) to Weirton, WV
panhandle.region: west of Pittsburgh through SW PA into WV Northern Panhandle
panhandle.history: Former Panhandle Division of the Pennsylvania Railroad (Pittsburgh to Cincinnati/Chicago/St Louis), closed 1991, tracks removed 1996. 100th successful PA rail-trail project.
panhandle.note: Named for WV's narrow Northern Panhandle it crosses. Mostly exposed/unshaded — bring sunscreen.

three_rivers_heritage.name: Three Rivers Heritage Trail
three_rivers_heritage.endpoints: Both banks of the Allegheny, Monongahela & Ohio rivers around Pittsburgh
connects: montour (via T rail to Bethel Park)

named_segments.north_shore: Along Ohio and Allegheny rivers north of downtown
named_segments.strip_district: South bank of Allegheny; food market district
named_segments.eliza_furnace: North bank of Mon; past former J&L furnace site; aka 'Jail Trail'
named_segments.south_side: South bank of Mon; access to Carson Street
named_segments.duck_hollow: Further up Mon toward Frick Park

three_rivers_heritage.history: Concept started 1990. First groundbreaking 1991. Completed incrementally. 60+ interpretive signs along route. Named National Recreation Trail.
three_rivers_heritage.note: Non-linear; 15 mph limit; sections shared with vehicle traffic. Over 500,000 users/year.

rachel_carson.name: Rachel Carson Trail
rachel_carson.surface: primitive: dirt singletrack, doubletrack, power/gas-line cuts, ~15% on roads
rachel_carson.grade: very steep, few switchbacks; ~6,600-6,970 ft cumulative one-way
rachel_carson.endpoints: North Park to Harrison Hills Park (NE Allegheny County)
rachel_carson.history: Built by volunteers 1972–1975 on former Baker Trail segment. Named for conservationist Rachel Carson, born in Springdale PA.
rachel_carson.note: NO bikes. Day-use only (no camping, no shelters). Yellow blazes. Follow blazes over GPS — many reroutes. ~15% (6.8 mi) on roads; some have dangerous traffic.

seasonal_notes.spring: Trail turns muddy after rain — avoid wet periods. Blowdowns frequent before spring clearing. Wildflowers in wooded sections.
seasonal_notes.summer: Bugs and heat on exposed power-line cuts. Poison ivy at peak. Annual Challenge hike 3rd Saturday of June.
seasonal_notes.fall: Prime hiking: Oct foliage, cooler temps, bugs gone. Deer archery season starts Oct 3 — wear orange.
seasonal_notes.winter: Trail passable but road sections can be icy. No hunting Sundays now legal in PA so year-round. Mud if above-freezing.

pois.name: National Tunnel (CLOSED – check status)
pois.desc: CLOSED since November 2025. Ceiling deterioration found at the western entrance during a safety inspection. Full repair cost: ~$2.75 million. Construction won't begin until early 2027 at the earliest, pending funding. Alternate access: McConnell Road trailhead (Mile 24.7) heading west; Kurnick trailhead (Mile 25.6) heading east. The trail remains navigable on either side of the closure; only the tunnel itself is blocked.
pois.tip: In winter, freezer-style doors kept it above freezing to prevent ice — fascinating engineering detail.
pois.correction_note: Updated to reflect confirmed 2027 timeline. The $2.75M repair campaign is underway. | [2026-06 coord audit] Coordinates corrected from [40.479,-80.29] (near Coraopolis, ~22mi off) to MM25 Cecil Twp per Wikipedia/MTC. Was wrongly labeled Findlay Twp (that is the Enlow Tunnel at MM7).
pois.confidence_note: Confirmed closed, 2027 timeline, fundraising active — nationaltunnel.org and Observer-Reporter May 2026.

pois.name: Enlow Tunnel
pois.desc: 575-ft tunnel near the northern end, set in a scenic wooded valley alongside a stream. The journey through the valley on either side is as enjoyable as the tunnel itself.
pois.confidence_note: Permanent physical structure or natural feature; existence verified by trail authority.

pois.name: Greer Tunnel & Chartiers Creek High Bridge
pois.desc: 235-ft tunnel built in 1913 flanked by the Chartiers Creek High Bridge on the west — the highest bridge on the trail — and another bridge on the east that crosses over an active railroad (look down to see a second tunnel below). One of the most scenic 2-mile stretches on the trail. Recently fully renovated ($1M+ project, reopened May 2024).
pois.tip: Look down from the east bridge to see trains below. There's also an X-1 railroad service crane preserved nearby — a rare surviving piece of Montour Railroad equipment.
pois.confidence_note: Confirmed reopened May 10, 2024 — ribbon cutting documented by RACW. Coordinate approximate (derived from MM29 marker; tunnel is "near MM29" per trail guides).
pois.correction_note: [2026-06 coord audit] Coordinates corrected from [40.311,-80.181] (which sat at the National Tunnel location, MM25) to ~MM29; mile 28.5 -> 29.

pois.name: McDonald Viaduct (Trestle)
pois.desc: 960-ft (nearly 1,000 ft) bridge, 75 ft above the valley — the longest bridge on the trail. Crosses over Noblestown Road, Robinson Run, AND the Panhandle Trail far below. Reopened 2003 after major renovation. A 'wow moment' even for experienced trail riders.
pois.tip: Deck narrows on the bridge — watch the trail, not just the view. You can see the Panhandle Trail through the structure below.
pois.confidence_note: Permanent physical structure or natural feature; existence verified by trail authority.

pois.name: Library Viaduct
pois.desc: 506-ft railroad trestle over Library Rd (PA-88) and light rail tracks. A $2.2M renovation project. Navigation becomes challenging around Library junction — the on-road stretch around this viaduct was historically where cyclists would give up.
pois.tip: This is the infamous navigation gap. Signage drops out near Library. Know the route before you get here.
pois.confidence_note: Permanent physical structure or natural feature; existence verified by trail authority.

pois.name: X-1 Railroad Service Crane
pois.locator: ~Mile 28-29, Peters Township (West Peters area near Greer Tunnel)
pois.desc: A former Montour Railroad working service crane, preserved trailside. One of a handful of surviving pieces of original Montour Railroad equipment — a great history stop right in the densest scenic section of the trail.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: The Tandem Connection (bike shop, snacks, ice cream)
pois.desc: The trail's essential shop, housed in a century-old mining company store building. Bike rentals (including tandems and fat-tire winter bikes), parts, repairs, and coffee in the morning. Perry's Ice Cream with wild flavors (Bittersweet Sinphony, Deep Sea Treasure, Fool's Gold) — a beloved finish-line treat. The only full bike shop directly on the trail.
pois.tip: The shop's in a 100-year-old building once used as a coal company store. Also sells Montour Trail souvenirs.
pois.confidence_note: Business with verified website; hours and status may change seasonally. Call ahead.
pois.correction_note: [2026-06 coord audit] Coordinates corrected from [40.269,-80.198] (~3mi off-trail) to the Hendersonville trailhead at MM27 (parking GPS 40.2996,-80.152).

pois.name: McDonald trail town
pois.desc: A pleasant small trail town with a charming brick downtown (built during the oil boom of the late 1800s). Pizzerias, a Subway, and a beloved ice cream stand with legendary $1.85 sky-high soft-serve. The restored old train station is worth walking through.
pois.confidence_note: Food stop from trail guide sources; not independently verified as still open in 2025-2026.
pois.correction_note: [2026-06 coord audit] Stated mile corrected 20 -> 17 (McDonald is ~MM17). Coordinates were already correct.

pois.name: 317 E. Lincoln Ave diner, McDonald
pois.desc: Homemade food with big portions at reasonable prices — a local favorite post-ride sit-down spot.
pois.confidence_note: Food stop from trail guide sources; not independently verified as still open in 2025-2026.

pois.name: Vintage caboose & old-sign warehouse
pois.desc: A restored Norfolk Southern caboose beside a warehouse covered in vintage brand signage from decades past — an unexpected, photo-worthy railroad-history stop that appears out of nowhere.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Six Little Free Libraries
pois.desc: Six book-swap boxes installed at points along the route. Grab a book, leave one, read on a bench.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Horse ranch & pastoral views near Library
pois.desc: After navigating the tricky Library section you're rewarded with sweeping rural Americana views over a working horse ranch.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Montour Run creek corridor
pois.desc: The trail follows Montour Run (the creek it's named for) for its entire northern 8.5 miles from Coraopolis — leafy, stream-side riding through neighborhoods sprinkled with wildflowers.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Wildflower species along the trail
pois.desc: Blue cohosh, butterfly weed, chicory, snow trillium, trumpet creeper, and wild bergamot appear in season. Fall leaf color is spectacular in October.
pois.tip: October is prime fall color. Spring brings trillium early — look in the shadier wooded sections.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Deer (especially at dusk)
pois.desc: White-tailed deer are extremely common, especially near dusk. The Bethel Branch wooded corridor has frequent deer crossing the path. Also hawks gliding over the Westland Branch farmlands.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Westland Branch — rural farmland & tank-train watching
pois.desc: Quiet rural branch trail through open farmland with panoramic valley views — a completely different character from the main trail. An active rail line runs alongside carrying slow MarkWest natural gas tank trains at <15 mph, close enough to wave to the crew. Hawks overhead, cattle in pastures below.
pois.tip: The tank trains are a genuine novelty — creeping gas-industry freights within arm's reach of the trail.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Bethel Branch — highest elevation, T-rail access
pois.locator: 2.8-mi branch from Peters Township into Bethel Park
pois.desc: Heavily forested residential branch reaching the trail's highest elevation. Notable because it brings you close to the end of the South Hills T light-rail line — you can park downtown and ride the T to the trail (Port Authority allows 2 bikes per car).
pois.confidence_note: Sourced from official trail authority page; generally current but conditions change.

pois.name: Abundant water fountains & rest areas
pois.desc: Praised by long-distance riders as having more water stops than almost any comparable trail. Benches and tables frequent.
pois.confidence_note: Sourced from official trail authority page; generally current but conditions change.

pois.name: Two trail-side campgrounds
pois.desc: Primitive camping right on the trail — not car camping. Boggs near Imperial, Cecil-Henderson near Hendersonville. Good for multi-day tours or bike-packers.
pois.confidence_note: Sourced from official trail authority page; generally current but conditions change.

pois.name: Large bike shop at Hendersonville trailhead
pois.desc: A large bicycle shop at the Hendersonville trailhead — full-service repairs, restrooms, and parking.
pois.confidence_note: Sourced from official trail authority page; generally current but conditions change.

pois.name: Pittsburgh T light rail access
pois.desc: Take the Port Authority T (light rail) from Downtown Pittsburgh to the South Hills Village or Library stations, then ride ~0.7 mi to the Montour. Port Authority allows 2 bikes per railcar.
pois.confidence_note: Business with verified website; confirm details directly before relying on.

pois.name: Route 51 crossing — navigation gap
pois.desc: At the Route 51 crossing (a busy 4-lane road with no shoulders) signage disappears. Cross at the light and walk your bike on the grass berm. A short on-road section also passes through a narrow single-lane road tunnel in this area.
pois.tip: A sidewalk is being developed to fix this; verify current status. Know this section before you get to it.
pois.confidence_note: Information from secondary trail sources; generally reliable but not independently verified in this session.

pois.name: Library navigation gap
pois.desc: Trail signage disappears in the Library area around the viaduct. Historically where cyclists give up and turn back.
pois.confidence_note: Information from secondary trail sources; generally reliable but not independently verified in this session.

pois.name: Clairton Connector — GAP link (partially on road)
pois.desc: The connection from Clairton toward the GAP currently includes several miles of on-road riding. Not a seamless trail link yet.
pois.confidence_note: Information from secondary trail sources; generally reliable but not independently verified in this session.

pois.name: Rough first mile from Coraopolis
pois.desc: The first mile out of Coraopolis has a rough surface (potholes). It improves after that but stay alert.
pois.confidence_note: Information from secondary trail sources; generally reliable but not independently verified in this session.

pois.name: Point State Park & Fort Pitt
pois.desc: The bronze GAP medallion is set next to the famous three-rivers fountain. The Fort Pitt Blockhouse in the park is the oldest surviving European structure west of the Alleghenies. The Fort Pitt Museum tells the story of early Pittsburgh and the French & Indian War. Historic Fort Duquesne outlines are set into the ground nearby.
pois.confidence_note: Government agency or federally designated site; highly reliable.

pois.name: Carrie Furnace (Rivers of Steel)
pois.desc: The last surviving blast furnace complex from Pittsburgh's steel era, visible across the Monongahela. Tours run seasonally by Rivers of Steel. An eerie, photogenic industrial ruin that looms over the trail.
pois.confidence_note: Verified permanent site operated by government or established nonprofit.

pois.name: Homestead Strike Pumphouse & historic marker
pois.desc: Historical marker at the Carnegie Steel pumphouse where the infamous 1892 Homestead Strike confrontation between steelworkers and Pinkerton agents happened at night. One of the most significant labor events in US history.
pois.confidence_note: Historical site with documented records; physical remains or markers present.

pois.name: The Waterfront (Homestead) + steel stacks
pois.desc: A large retail/restaurant district built on the former site of the world's largest steel mill. Surviving smokestacks from the old Carnegie Steel plant frame the modern shops. Includes major chain restaurants plus boutique spots.
pois.confidence_note: Business with verified website; hours and status may change seasonally. Call ahead.

pois.name: Renziehausen Park rose garden
pois.desc: 1,800 rose bushes in a trailside park — a surprising burst of botanical beauty in an industrial-heritage stretch.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Kennywood roller coaster views
pois.desc: The trail winds directly past the back fence of Kennywood amusement park. You get close-up views of the Thunderbolt and Phantom's Revenge roller coasters and can hear riders screaming. A surreal moment on a trail ride.
pois.confidence_note: Well-documented Pittsburgh-area landmark; regularly verified by local sources.

pois.name: Bald eagle nest (visible from trail)
pois.desc: A well-known bald eagle nest visible from the trail on the river side. Also look for an osprey nest before the McKeesport bridge crossing.
pois.tip: Easier to spot in fall when leaves thin. The GAP between McKeesport and Pittsburgh is one of the best eagle/osprey sections on the trail.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Edgar Thomson Works (last active steel mill)
pois.desc: The last operating steel mill in the Pittsburgh area, visible across the Monongahela from the trail — a striking contrast to the recreational trail you're riding on.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Dead Man's Hollow Conservation Area
pois.desc: 450 acres of forest — the largest privately-protected green space in Allegheny County — with 8+ miles of hiking trails branching off the GAP. Contains the ruins of a former terracotta pipe factory, Tabletop Rock, and Catfish Pond. Spooky name, gorgeous trails.
pois.confidence_note: Verified permanent site operated by government or established nonprofit.

pois.name: The Red Waterfall (acid mine drainage)
pois.locator: Between McKeesport and West Newton (~Mile 120 area)
pois.desc: Rocks stained vivid red-orange by water runoff from old mines — an eerie beautiful example of the industrial past's lingering environmental legacy. The color is acid mine drainage; striking from the trail but a reminder of the cost of coal.
pois.tip: Best viewed after rain when flow is higher. Safe to view from the trail — don't wade in it.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Dravo Cemetery & Queen Aliquippa Camp
pois.desc: A historic cemetery with graves dating to 1812 sits trailside. Queen Aliquippa Camp nearby has lean-to shelters, bathrooms, and a hand-pump water source for overnight riders.
pois.confidence_note: Historical site with documented records; physical remains or markers present.

pois.name: Sutersville ice cream
pois.desc: Ice cream just off-trail near the Sutersville trailhead — a beloved stop reported by numerous riders. The shop has changed hands; look it up before you go.
pois.confidence_note: Specific business not named; 'ice cream in Sutersville' may or may not still exist.

pois.name: Miners Sky Ferry marker — Jacobs Creek
pois.desc: A historical marker for the 'Miners Sky Ferry' — an aerial gondola that shuttled six miners at a time across the Youghiogheny River to reach the mines on the opposite bank. One of the GAP's most fascinating, little-known history stops.
pois.confidence_note: Historical site with documented records; physical remains or markers present.

pois.name: Sager Mosaics & The Ruins Project
pois.desc: The Ruins Project: a permanent mosaic art installation inside the ruins of Banning No. 2 coal mine (founded 1891, Fayette County). Rachel Sager bought the abandoned mine in 2015; 500+ artists from 17+ countries have contributed. Guided tours ~90 minutes outdoors, $30/person, reservation required at sagermosaics.com. Season: mid-April through Thanksgiving. The GAP's interpretive highlight — the coal story told not in a museum case but in the walls themselves. Closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays.
pois.tip: Book the tour in advance at sagermosaics.com. Tours run mid-April through Thanksgiving. The studio is always open walk-in. One of the most memorable stops on the entire 335-mile Pittsburgh-to-DC route.
pois.correction_note: Tour price confirmed $30/person (sourced from sagermosaics.com/studio). 11+ year project, 500+ artists confirmed.
pois.confidence_note: Active site confirmed with 2026 Harvard event and highland-outdoors.com article March 2026.

pois.name: West Newton
pois.desc: The first real trail town south of Pittsburgh and a popular overnight. The Trailside Pub & Restaurant is literally right on the trail (burgers, wings, brews, famous strawberry salad). West Newton Bicycle Shop upstairs for tune-ups and rentals. Bloom Brew for craft beer. West Newton Station Visitor Center with restrooms and parking. Historic 1870s downtown across the century-old bridge.
pois.confidence_note: Food stop from trail guide sources; not independently verified as still open in 2025-2026.

pois.name: Cedar Creek Park
pois.desc: County park where the GAP passes through. A gorge trail in the northern section gives stunning ravine views. Popular paddleboarding take-out. Wildflowers and fungi in season.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Connellsville
pois.desc: The largest city on the GAP between Pittsburgh and Cumberland, once home to more coal millionaires per capita than any other US city. Kickstand Kitchen for creative salads and sandwiches. Colebrook Chocolate Co. for ice cream and fancy chocolates. Amtrak stop here — the only one between Pittsburgh and Cumberland, making it a logical start/end for partial trips.
pois.tip: Connellsville Bed and Breakfast sits above a wine store just off the trail — convenient with bike storage.
pois.confidence_note: Food stop from trail guide sources; not independently verified as still open in 2025-2026.

pois.name: Olympic Oak at Connellsville High School
pois.desc: John Woodruff, a local sprinter, won the 800m gold medal at the 1936 Berlin Olympics. Hitler presented him with a sapling. That oak tree — the 'Olympic Oak' — still grows at Connellsville High School, a 15-minute detour from the trail.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Youghiogheny River Gorge (Connellsville to Ohiopyle)
pois.desc: The GAP threads Pennsylvania's deepest gorge for ~16 miles. Rocky outcroppings, boulder-strewn banks, and the 'Yawk' rushing below — considered one of the most scenic stretches of the trail.
pois.confidence_note: Permanent physical structure or natural feature; existence verified by trail authority.

pois.name: Great Passage Falls
pois.desc: A ~15-ft trailside plunge waterfall — one of the first of several waterfalls in this stretch. Has a bench and short path down. Best with good rainfall; can run dry late summer.
pois.confidence_note: Permanent physical structure or natural feature; existence verified by trail authority.

pois.name: Johnson Run Falls
pois.desc: A hillside cascade series totaling ~45–50 ft, visible in the woods at the edge of Ohiopyle State Park. Best in spring.
pois.confidence_note: Natural feature; location well-documented on trail maps.

pois.name: Ohiopyle (trail town & state park)
pois.desc: The trail's most spectacular town — set in 19,000 acres of state park. Watch Class III-IV whitewater rafters from the Low and High Bridges over the Yough. Old Western Maryland Railway station serves as a visitor center. Ice cream shops, outfitters, miniature golf, and the Meadow Run natural waterslides. Falls City Pub for food and beer.
pois.confidence_note: Site with official website; physical features unlikely to change but verify hours/access.

pois.name: Ohiopyle Falls
pois.desc: A wide 20-ft cascade on the Yough right in the center of town — unmissable and one of the best photo stops on the entire trail.
pois.confidence_note: Permanent physical structure or natural feature; existence verified by trail authority.

pois.name: Cucumber Falls
pois.desc: A photogenic 36-ft plunge fall — easily accessible and at its most dramatic in spring when flow peaks.
pois.confidence_note: Permanent physical structure or natural feature; existence verified by trail authority.

pois.name: Meadow Run Cascades & Natural Waterslides
pois.desc: A medium waterfall leading into a series of small cascades over natural rock — locals and families use it as a natural water park. Free and accessible from the trail.
pois.confidence_note: Natural feature; location well-documented on trail maps.

pois.name: Jonathan Run Falls
pois.desc: A gentle cascade over rock ledges reached by a short hike off the trail into the park.
pois.confidence_note: Natural feature; location well-documented on trail maps.

pois.name: Ferncliff Peninsula (hike spur)
pois.desc: An easy 1.7-mile loop trail through a National Natural Landmark peninsula formed by a dramatic U-bend in the Yough. Rich biodiversity, tree-shrouded river views, and wild rhododendrons.
pois.confidence_note: Permanent physical structure or natural feature; existence verified by trail authority.

pois.name: Fallingwater & Kentuck Knob (side trips by shuttle/car)
pois.locator: Near Mill Run (~Mile 80), 8 mi from Ohiopyle
pois.desc: Frank Lloyd Wright's masterpiece, built over a waterfall — widely considered the greatest work of American architecture. Kentuck Knob, another Wright house, is 10 minutes away. Both require a car or shuttle from Ohiopyle, but neither should be skipped if you're in the area.
pois.tip: Book Fallingwater tickets well in advance. Wilderness Voyageurs offers bike-to-Fallingwater shuttles from Ohiopyle.
pois.confidence_note: Site with official website; physical features unlikely to change but verify hours/access.

pois.name: Confluence
pois.desc: Where the Yough, Casselman, and Laurel Hill Creek all meet below mountain ridges — a natural crossroads. Lucky Dog Cafe has good food and atmosphere despite a modest exterior. River's Edge B&B for lodging with river-porch dining. A local bike shop has helped many a flat-tired rider. Town square with gazebo; annual Pumpkin Fest.
pois.confidence_note: Business with verified website; hours and status may change seasonally. Call ahead.

pois.name: Pinkerton Tunnel, High & Low Bridges
pois.desc: 849-ft tunnel sandwiched between two bridges over the Casselman River at a dramatic river bend (Pinkerton Neck). The 'Pinkerton High Bridge' and 'Pinkerton Low Bridge' offer views that rival Ohiopyle's double bridges. A jaw-dropping 3-in-1 engineering and scenic moment.
pois.confidence_note: Documented on gaptrail.org; tunnel reopened 2015.

pois.name: Small waterfall near Garrett (bench stop)
pois.desc: A small trailside waterfall right next to a bench — a perfect place to pause and breathe on the uphill toward the Divide.
pois.confidence_note: Permanent physical structure or natural feature; existence verified by trail authority.

pois.name: Rockwood
pois.desc: Mill Shoppes in a restored 19th-century lumber mill and opera house serving pizza, wine, ice cream, and local chocolates. Hostel on Main for budget overnight (secure bike storage, showers, laundry). A beautiful trailside mural and art overlook near the parking area.
pois.confidence_note: Food stop from trail guide sources; not independently verified as still open in 2025-2026.

pois.name: Keystone Viaduct
pois.desc: 910-ft trestle over Flaugherty Creek — a graceful bridge that winds through the treetops on the climb toward the Continental Divide. Two viaducts bracket Meyersdale, with Keystone on the south and the larger Salisbury Viaduct to the north.
pois.confidence_note: Permanent physical structure or natural feature; existence verified by trail authority.

pois.name: Meyersdale — PA Maple Capital
pois.desc: The highest trail town (and noticeably cooler — pack a layer). Famous for maple syrup. Donges Drive-In for maple milkshakes. Levi Deal Mansion B&B is one of the finest stays on the entire GAP. Morguen Toole Co for dinner. Meyersdale train station has a small railroad museum, restrooms, and a caboose.
pois.confidence_note: Food stop from trail guide sources; not independently verified as still open in 2025-2026.

pois.name: Salisbury Viaduct
pois.desc: The longest and most dramatic bridge on the GAP — 1,908 ft across the Casselman River valley at 101 ft high. Sunrise or sunset here with the maple farms spread below is one of the finest views on the entire Pittsburgh-to-DC route.
pois.confidence_note: Visible, permanent structure; 1,908 ft length is documented.

pois.name: Bollman Truss Bridge, Meyersdale
pois.desc: One of only two surviving cast-iron truss bridges in North America — an extremely rare surviving piece of 19th-century railroad engineering right on the trail.
pois.confidence_note: Bollman Truss Bridge in Meyersdale is a documented National Historic Landmark — one of 2 surviving in North America.

pois.name: Eastern Continental Divide
pois.desc: The trail's highest point. Historical murals depict the region's history and a map shows the trail's elevation profile through the mountain. From here, it's all downhill toward Pittsburgh — a psychological milestone on any through-ride.
pois.confidence_note: The Continental Divide is a geographic fact.

pois.name: Big Savage Tunnel
pois.desc: 3,291-ft tunnel through Big Savage Mountain — fully lit. The eastern portal opens to a picnic area and four-state overlook. One of the most dramatic tunnel exits anywhere. CLOSED December to early April.
pois.tip: CLOSED roughly Dec–early April. The eastern portal bench may be one of the best viewpoints on the entire Pittsburgh-to-DC route. Bring lights for the 3,300-ft tunnel.
pois.confidence_note: Confirmed via gaptrail.org and TrailLink; Apr 4, 2026 confirmed open per TrailLink review.

pois.name: Birdwatching highlights trail-wide
pois.desc: Ospreys and bald eagles nesting near McKeesport/Pittsburgh (mile 132–142). Herons and mallards along the quieter Yough sections near West Newton. Warblers and woodpeckers throughout Ohiopyle SP (one of PA's top 100 bird-watching sites). Red-winged blackbirds, ruffed grouse, and eastern towhees near meadows around Meyersdale. Swallows and swifts near Cumberland.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Fishing — trophy trout and bass
pois.desc: Nine-mile all-tackle trophy trout section of the Yough through Ohiopyle SP (closed early April until season opener). Casselman River between Rockwood and Confluence — stocked trout and smallmouth bass. Flaugherty Creek (Divide to Meyersdale) — stocked trout. Laurel Hill Creek near Confluence — stocked rainbow, brown, and wild brook trout.
pois.tip: Yough trophy trout section: Ramcat Run to the Rt. 381 bridge, 9 miles. PA fishing license + trout permit required. Fall is prime for large brown trout. The Yough stays cold year-round below the Confluence dam.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Wildlife along the GAP (general)
pois.desc: White-tailed deer, wild turkeys, and black bears (in the Ohiopyle/Laurel Highlands sections). River otters and mink on the Yough and Casselman. Rattlesnakes occasionally sun on the trail in the mountain sections (between Rockwood and the Divide). Wildflowers and spring waterfalls peak April–May; fall foliage peak mid-October near the divide.
pois.tip: Rattlesnakes are uncommon but not unheard of — give them space and they'll move on. One memorable trail report describes having to shoo one off the trail with a stick near the Casselman.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Ride direction & grade (planning)
pois.desc: The trail climbs ~1,800 ft from Pittsburgh to the Eastern Continental Divide, then drops ~1,800 ft to Cumberland. Riding Pittsburgh→Cumberland is 'mostly uphill then downhill'. Riding Cumberland→Pittsburgh is 'long climb then long coast'. Many experienced riders do Cumberland→Pittsburgh for the long downhill stretch.
pois.confidence_note: Sourced from official trail authority page; generally current but conditions change.

pois.name: Amtrak stops near the GAP
pois.desc: Connellsville (Mile 88) and Cumberland (Mile 0) both have Amtrak service, enabling one-way trip logistics. Bikes can travel on the Capitol Limited with a reservation.
pois.confidence_note: Government agency or federally designated site; highly reliable.

pois.name: Helicon Brewing (trailside brewery)
pois.locator: Oakdale, PA — directly on the trail
pois.desc: A highly regarded craft brewery right alongside the trail with a rotating tap list, food trucks, and live music in summer. A go-to mid-ride or post-ride stop. Dog friendly.
pois.confidence_note: Business with verified website; hours and status may change seasonally. Call ahead.

pois.name: Fossil Cliff
pois.desc: Cross a pedestrian bridge over Robinson Run to cliff outcroppings where fossilized fern leaves are embedded in the rock face. A geological detour you wouldn't expect on a flat rail trail.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: McDonald Trail Station & History Center
pois.desc: Parking, restrooms, info center, trail maps, vending machines, and picnic tables. Right at the junction with the Montour Trail connector. History Center documents the Panhandle Railroad's role as a Pittsburgh-to-St. Louis connector.
pois.confidence_note: Practical info from trail guides; verify current status before visit.

pois.name: PCC&StL Railroad marker
pois.desc: An old concrete marker stamped with the initials of the Pittsburgh, Cincinnati, Chicago & St. Louis Railway — a remnant from the 1860s railroad merger that built this corridor. A history-buff photo stop.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Burgettstown food & trail station
pois.desc: Near-trail market, diner, and pizza (Walden's Restaurant among others). Trail station with restrooms. The town's name comes from an early settler family.
pois.confidence_note: Generic food mention without specific business. Low specificity.

pois.name: Robinson Run creek crossing (6 times)
pois.desc: The trail crosses Robinson Run six times in the final stretch toward the eastern end — pleasant creekside riding and good bird watching along the stream.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Memorials, birdhouses & quarry ponds
pois.desc: Decorative birdhouses, a memorial to an avid runner, a children's 9/11 memorial, and old quarry ponds dot the eastern end near Walkers Mill.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Bulger high point
pois.desc: The trail's highest elevation. From Weirton it's a steady 16-mile climb to Bulger before the long descent each direction. The only significant climb on this otherwise relatively flat trail.
pois.confidence_note: Information from secondary trail sources; generally reliable but not independently verified in this session.

pois.name: WV/PA state line crossing
pois.desc: The trail crosses from WV into PA at the confluence of Harmon Creek and Paris Run, marked by a white rail sign. Many bridge crossings over the creek in the WV section make for nice photo opportunities, especially in fall.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Montour Trail connector
pois.desc: A ~1-mile connector links the Panhandle to the Montour Trail and onward (via the Montour) toward the GAP and Pittsburgh/DC.
pois.confidence_note: Sourced from official trail authority page; generally current but conditions change.

pois.name: Wildflowers & seasonal notes
pois.desc: Spring wildflowers line the trail especially in Washington County. Fall leaf color is good in the wooded WV and eastern PA sections. The trail is largely exposed/unshaded — bring sunscreen in summer and plan around overcast days for comfortable riding.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Sun exposure (practical warning)
pois.desc: Long stretches are completely unshaded and can get extremely hot. Overcast days are ideal for summer riding. Full sunscreen and extra water are essential.
pois.confidence_note: Information from secondary trail sources; generally reliable but not independently verified in this session.

pois.name: Point State Park fountain (GAP start)
pois.desc: The bronze GAP medallion next to the three-rivers fountain marks the start of the 335-mile Pittsburgh-to-Washington route. The park sits at the confluence of the Allegheny, Monongahela, and Ohio rivers.
pois.confidence_note: Government agency or federally designated site; highly reliable.

pois.name: Fort Duquesne Bridge crossing
pois.desc: Cross the Fort Duquesne Bridge to get the North Shore trail. Best downtown Pittsburgh skyline view from the bridge.
pois.confidence_note: Permanent physical structure or natural feature; existence verified by trail authority.

pois.name: PNC Park & Acrisure Stadium views
pois.desc: The trail runs directly in front of both stadiums. Best views of the Pittsburgh skyline from across the Allegheny River. The North Shore segment offers continuous river-and-downtown panoramas.
pois.confidence_note: Well-documented Pittsburgh-area landmark; regularly verified by local sources.

pois.name: Carnegie Science Center
pois.desc: The science center marks the point where the Allegheny meets the Ohio. A good turnaround for a city loop, with views back toward downtown.
pois.confidence_note: Well-documented Pittsburgh-area landmark; regularly verified by local sources.

pois.name: Andy Warhol Museum
pois.desc: Museum dedicated to Pittsburgh's most famous artist. Set in a former industrial building one block off the trail on the North Side.
pois.confidence_note: Well-documented Pittsburgh-area landmark; regularly verified by local sources.

pois.name: Herr's Island (Washington's Landing)
pois.locator: Allegheny River, off the North Side trail
pois.desc: A mostly residential island accessible via a historic railroad trestle off the trail. A quiet loop around the island with river views and a waterfront restaurant (Redfin Blue). The island was renamed 'Washington's Landing' because George Washington's raft capsized here in 1753, stranding him overnight during the French and Indian War.
pois.tip: Cyclists asked to ride slowly here and dismount near the marina. Peaceful contrast to the stadium bustle.
pois.confidence_note: Well-documented Pittsburgh-area landmark; regularly verified by local sources.

pois.name: Allegheny Landing outdoor sculpture park
pois.desc: One of the country's first urban riverfront sculpture parks — outdoor sculptures right along the trail with skyline backdrops.
pois.confidence_note: Well-documented Pittsburgh-area landmark; regularly verified by local sources.

pois.name: Fred Rogers Memorial Statue
pois.desc: The beloved 'Mister Rogers Neighborhood' star grew up near Pittsburgh. The memorial statue offers a great dusk photo with the downtown skyline across the river.
pois.confidence_note: Well-documented Pittsburgh-area landmark; regularly verified by local sources.

pois.name: Heinz Lofts (historic H.J. Heinz factory)
pois.desc: The former H.J. Heinz Company production buildings converted to loft apartments — registered historic landmarks. Ketchup was made here. The buildings still carry the old Heinz signage.
pois.confidence_note: Well-documented Pittsburgh-area landmark; regularly verified by local sources.

pois.name: Grist House Craft Brewery, Millvale
pois.desc: A beloved Pittsburgh craft brewery a few blocks off the trail in Millvale. Barrel tables, large outdoor family-friendly space, and rotating taps. Gets very busy on weekends.
pois.correction_note: Fixed website URL: was 'grissthouse.com' (wrong), now 'gristhouse.com' (confirmed correct). Hours: closed Mon-Tue; Wed-Fri 4-10pm; Sat 12-10pm; Sun 12-8pm. No kitchen — food trucks on site.
pois.confidence_note: Confirmed open via Yelp (updated June 2026). URL corrected. Hours verified.

pois.name: Duquesne Incline
pois.desc: An 1877 cable car (funicular) that climbs ~400 ft to the Mount Washington overlook in ~2 minutes. The quintessential Pittsburgh panorama — best at sunset or night. The Monongahela Incline is 0.3 mi east at Station Square for a second option.
pois.tip: The Mount Washington overlook is the most photographed view in Pittsburgh. Go at golden hour.
pois.confidence_note: Well-documented Pittsburgh-area landmark; regularly verified by local sources.

pois.name: Station Square
pois.locator: South bank of Mon, near Duquesne Incline
pois.desc: The old Pittsburgh & Lake Erie Railroad complex (1870s) repurposed into restaurants, bars, and shops. Good mix of casual and upscale dining right off the trail.
pois.confidence_note: Business with verified website; hours and status may change seasonally. Call ahead.

pois.name: Smithfield Street Bridge (1883)
pois.desc: An 1883 lenticular truss bridge with striking blue steel arches — a National Engineering Landmark. The trail passes beneath it; crossing it to Station Square gives great trail views.
pois.confidence_note: Permanent physical structure or natural feature; existence verified by trail authority.

pois.name: South Side / Carson Street bars & restaurants
pois.locator: South Side (South Side Riverfront trail section)
pois.desc: East Carson Street — a National Register Historic District — runs parallel to the trail and has Pittsburgh's densest bar and restaurant strip. SouthSide Works nearby has major chains plus a kayak launch. Easy trail access.
pois.confidence_note: Food stop from trail guide sources; not independently verified as still open in 2025-2026.

pois.name: Hot Metal Bridge
pois.locator: Connecting South Side to Second Ave / GAP link
pois.desc: A 1900 steel trestle that once carried molten iron across the river — now a pedestrian/bike bridge with remarkable views. Just across is the Hofbräuhaus outdoor terrace. This is also the access point to the Great Allegheny Passage.
pois.confidence_note: Well-documented Pittsburgh-area landmark; regularly verified by local sources.

pois.name: Hofbräuhaus Pittsburgh
pois.locator: Near Hot Metal Bridge, SouthSide Works
pois.desc: German beer hall with a large outdoor terrace overlooking the river. Start or end a trail ride here.
pois.confidence_note: Established regional business; website active; verify hours before visiting.

pois.name: Sly Fox at The Highline
pois.desc: A large brewpub right on the South Side trail with roll-up doors onto a 1-acre outdoor patio, 20 rotating taps, pub food, and live music weekends. Bike parking provided.
pois.confidence_note: Established regional business; website active; verify hours before visiting.

pois.name: Color Park (Baron Batch graffiti art zone)
pois.desc: A legal graffiti mural zone converted from an industrial lot — started by Pittsburgh artist Baron Batch. Always changing; something new each visit.
pois.confidence_note: Well-documented Pittsburgh-area landmark; regularly verified by local sources.

pois.name: 'The Workers' steel sculpture
pois.locator: Near Birmingham Bridge, South Side Riverfront Park
pois.desc: Two 18-ft steelworker figures built from scrap metal from local mills — honoring Pittsburgh's industrial past. An imposing and moving public art piece.
pois.confidence_note: Well-documented Pittsburgh-area landmark; regularly verified by local sources.

pois.name: SouthSide Works
pois.desc: A large mixed-use development with major retailers (REI, etc.), restaurants (Cheesecake Factory and others), and a kayak launch. Easy trail access via bridge from the South Side trail.
pois.confidence_note: Business with verified website; hours and status may change seasonally. Call ahead.

pois.name: Strip District food & spirits
pois.desc: Pittsburgh's renowned food market district with world cuisine, delis, and specialty food shops along Penn Ave. Just off the trail. Also local distilleries: Wigle Whiskey, Kingfly Spirits, Maggie's Farm Rum.
pois.tip: The 25th St trail access has been closed for construction at times — use Penn Ave bike lane as alternate.
pois.confidence_note: Food stop from trail guide sources; not independently verified as still open in 2025-2026.

pois.name: Thick Bikes (bike shop)
pois.desc: Full-service bike shop just off the South Side trail — tubes, parts, and repairs for trail emergencies.
pois.confidence_note: Business with verified website; confirm details directly before relying on.

pois.name: Schenley Park & Junction Hollow branch
pois.desc: 456-acre city park with trails, a café, golf, ice rink, swimming pool, and Phipps Conservatory & Botanical Gardens (stunning glass conservatory). The Junction Hollow branch leads from the Mon riverfront trail into the park, connecting to CMU and Pitt.
pois.confidence_note: Well-documented Pittsburgh-area landmark; regularly verified by local sources.

pois.name: Eliza Furnace / 'Jail Trail' stretch
pois.desc: The trail passes the site of J&L Steel's Eliza Furnaces and the Allegheny County Jail — a striking walk through layers of Pittsburgh history. The 'Jail Trail' nickname comes from the prison view.
pois.confidence_note: Well-documented Pittsburgh-area landmark; regularly verified by local sources.

pois.name: Hays bald eagle nest
pois.locator: Near Hays / Glenwood Bridge (~Mile 143 on the GAP portion)
pois.desc: A well-known nesting pair visible across the river. Best spotted fall through early spring when trees are bare.
pois.tip: The nest is on the far bank. Binoculars help. Fall visit recommended when leaves are down.
pois.confidence_note: Well-documented Pittsburgh-area landmark; regularly verified by local sources.

pois.name: Allegheny Goatscape goats
pois.desc: Herds of goats brought in seasonally to clear invasive vegetation along the riverbank. Not always present — a delightful surprise when you encounter them.
pois.confidence_note: Well-documented Pittsburgh-area landmark; regularly verified by local sources.

pois.name: 15-mile brewery loop route
pois.desc: A popular self-guided beer-loop: Sly Fox at The Highline (South Side) → Allegheny City Brewing (Deutschtown, North Side) → Grist House (Millvale) → Southern Tier (North Shore). ~15 miles; mostly on the trail with a few on-road blocks to reach the North Side breweries.
pois.tip: Requires a few on-road blocks to reach Deutschtown and Millvale breweries. Pedal responsibly.
pois.confidence_note: Food stop from trail guide sources; not independently verified as still open in 2025-2026.

pois.name: Shared-traffic sections (practical warning)
pois.desc: Some segments run on or beside vehicle traffic (dark red on the official trail map). The 15 mph trail speed limit applies throughout. Watch for gaps between trail segments, particularly near McKeesport and some construction zones.
pois.confidence_note: Information from secondary trail sources; generally reliable but not independently verified in this session.

pois.name: PBSC / Healthy Ride bike rentals
pois.desc: Dockless bike sharing available throughout Pittsburgh. An easy way for visitors to access the Three Rivers trail without bringing a bike.
pois.confidence_note: Business with verified website; confirm details directly before relying on.

pois.name: Trail character overview
pois.locator: Whole trail (North Park to Harrison Hills Park)
pois.desc: A rugged, primitive hiking trail (NO bikes) through northern Allegheny County. 46 miles, ~6,600–6,970 ft of cumulative one-way climb. Yellow-blazed but with multiple reroutes. Day-use only — no camping, no shelters. ~15% on roads. Many stream crossings. This is not a casual trail.
pois.confidence_note: Sourced from official trail authority page; generally current but conditions change.

pois.name: Wet stream crossings from the first step
pois.locator: North Park start and many points throughout
pois.desc: The very first feature at the North Park end is a stream crossing — you will get your feet wet. Multiple unavoidable crossings throughout. Bring appropriate footwear.
pois.confidence_note: Information from secondary trail sources; generally reliable but not independently verified in this session.

pois.name: ~6.8 miles on roads (some with fast traffic)
pois.desc: About 15% of the trail runs along roads. Most are lightly traveled but some carry fast-moving traffic with blind curves. The trail conservancy urges extreme caution and notes drivers often cannot see hikers.
pois.confidence_note: Information from secondary trail sources; generally reliable but not independently verified in this session.

pois.name: Notorious named climbs
pois.desc: The hardest climbs have names: Bull Creek Hill, Rich Hill, Log Cabin Hill, Murray Hill (erosion reroute in place), Crawford Run Hills. Each can drop or gain ~300 ft straight up and down without switchbacks. Loose shale makes descents treacherous.
pois.confidence_note: Information from secondary trail sources; generally reliable but not independently verified in this session.

pois.name: Mud and blowdowns after rain
pois.desc: The trail becomes nearly impassable during and after extended rain. Power-line cut sections especially get muddy. Also frequent blowdowns across the path.
pois.confidence_note: Information from secondary trail sources; generally reliable but not independently verified in this session.

pois.name: Poison ivy, nettles, knotweed & bugs
pois.desc: Heavy poison ivy, stinging nettles, and Japanese knotweed make off-trail footing hazardous. Bug pressure is high in summer. Bring bug spray and wear pants.
pois.confidence_note: Information from secondary trail sources; generally reliable but not independently verified in this session.

pois.name: Follow blazes not the GPS app
pois.desc: Multiple active reroutes (Bailies Run, Murray Hill, horse farm in Harrison Twp, a section past Burtner Rd). GPS trails on apps lag behind these reroutes. Follow yellow blazes on trees.
pois.confidence_note: Information from secondary trail sources; generally reliable but not independently verified in this session.

pois.name: Rachel Carson Homestead
pois.desc: The childhood home where Rachel Carson (author of Silent Spring, the book that launched the modern environmental movement) grew up. A short spur trail leads directly from the RCT to the house.
pois.confidence_note: Historical site with documented records; physical remains or markers present.

pois.name: Hartwood Acres Mansion spur
pois.desc: A spur off the RCT leads through the grounds of Hartwood Acres, a county park centered on a Tudor-revival mansion. Scenic pastoral views.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Harrison Hills Park (eastern terminus)
pois.locator: NE Allegheny County, above the Allegheny River
pois.desc: The eastern end of the trail features overlooks above the Allegheny River. A rewarding finish with river views after a long hard hike.
pois.confidence_note: Site from trail guide and map sources; physical existence reliable but operational details may vary.

pois.name: Annual Rachel Carson Trail Challenge (June)
pois.desc: 37-mile sunrise-to-sunset endurance hike (15 hrs). Most registered hikers don't finish. Shorter options: Homestead Challenge (19 mi) and an 8-mi option. It's a genuine community event — hundreds of hikers on the trail that day.
pois.confidence_note: Site with official website; physical features unlikely to change but verify hours/access.

pois.name: Sheetz at Creighton / Freeport Rd (resupply)
pois.desc: A major convenience store resupply point at roughly the halfway mark, used by Challenge hikers. Water, food, and supplies.
pois.confidence_note: Practical info from trail guides; verify current status before visit.

pois.name: Necromancer Brewing (post-hike reward)
pois.locator: Near North Park end (~7 mi from trailhead)
pois.desc: A dog-friendly brewery (BYO food / food trucks) — not on the trail itself but a popular post-RCT reward near the North Park trailhead end.
pois.confidence_note: Necromancer Brewing is an established Pittsburgh brewery; trail proximity claimed but not verified.

pois.name: North Park trailhead
pois.desc: Western trailhead with large parking at the spillway. The trail immediately climbs past the Pie Traynor baseball field and out of the park. Expect wet feet on the first stream crossing within 100 yards.
pois.confidence_note: Business with verified website; confirm details directly before relying on.

pois.name: Wildflower season: March–April (earliest ephemerals)
pois.locator: Shaded forest floors, creek drainages (Dead Man's Hollow, Cedar Creek Park, Ohiopyle, Rachel Carson Trail)
pois.desc: First wave: harbinger of spring, bloodroot (blooms days then drops petals), spring beauties. Snow trillium appears in very early April. Dutchman's breeches and Virginia bluebells just showing by late April. Best along creek drainages in wooded sections.
pois.tip: Bloodroot blooms for only a few days — if you see it, you're early-season lucky. Dead Man's Hollow and Cedar Creek Park along the GAP are especially good for these early species.
pois.confidence_note: Seasonal intel from secondary sources; verify actual dates before planning.

pois.name: Wildflower season: May (peak for most ephemerals)
pois.locator: Trail-wide; especially Ohiopyle gorge, GAP Connellsville-Ohiopyle corridor, Rachel Carson Trail wooded sections
pois.desc: Peak window for spring ephemerals. Red and white trillium, trout lilies, squirrel corn, blue-eyed Mary, dwarf larkspur (SW PA specialty), yellow violet, wild columbine. Virginia bluebells carpet hillsides in early May then vanish. Phipps Conservatory cites mid-April to mid-May as prime window for the Ohiopyle gorge area and the GAP's Ramcat Hollow section.
pois.tip: Mid-April to mid-May is the prime window for the GAP's Ohiopyle section. Great Gorge Trail at Ohiopyle: red and white trillium, trout lilies, Dutchman's breeches, and violets. Check WPC's website for real-time reports.
pois.confidence_note: Seasonal intel from secondary sources; verify actual dates before planning.

pois.name: Wildflower season: June–August (summer species)
pois.locator: Montour Trail open meadows, Panhandle Trail (exposed sections), GAP meadows near Meyersdale
pois.desc: Mountain laurel (PA state flower) peaks mid-June — look on the Bethel Branch and Rachel Carson Trail's wooded ridges. Rhododendron follows in early July. Wild bergamot, butterfly weed, chicory, trumpet creeper, golden ragwort through the summer. Wild columbine June–July.
pois.tip: Mountain laurel at its best on the RCT's ridgelines and the Bethel Branch. Rhododendron is spectacular in Ohiopyle State Park in early July — worth timing a visit around.
pois.confidence_note: Seasonal intel from secondary sources; verify actual dates before planning.

pois.name: Wildflower season: August–September (late summer)
pois.locator: Open meadows and power-line cuts; Rachel Carson Trail power-line sections; Panhandle Trail
pois.desc: Goldenrod, black-eyed Susan, coneflowers, wild asters. Second wildflower peak at Raccoon Creek State Park Wildflower Reserve late August to early September.
pois.confidence_note: Seasonal intel from secondary sources; verify actual dates before planning.

pois.name: Fall foliage: Early peak (early–mid October) — GAP high elevations
pois.locator: GAP high elevations: Eastern Continental Divide area, Somerset County, Meyersdale, Big Savage Mountain (MD/PA border)
pois.desc: High elevations (2,000+ ft) on the GAP color 2–3 weeks before Pittsburgh. Sugar and red maple, black cherry, and birch hit first. Mt. Davis area (near Meyersdale) often the first to peak. PA DCNR publishes weekly foliage reports starting September 1 — check pa.gov/dcnr for real-time tracking.
pois.tip: Early October: ride the GAP's Divide-to-Meyersdale section for peak maples. Mid-October: Laurel and Chestnut Ridges (Ohiopyle area). Late October: Pittsburgh metro and low-elevation Montour / Three Rivers sections.
pois.confidence_note: Seasonal intel from secondary sources; verify actual dates before planning.

pois.name: Fall foliage: Mid peak (mid–late October) — Ohiopyle & Laurel Highlands
pois.locator: Ohiopyle State Park, Connellsville-to-Ohiopyle gorge, Panhandle Trail wooded WV section
pois.desc: Mid-October is peak for the GAP's Ohiopyle section and the surrounding ridges. The gorge turns spectacular with maples and birches; the Yough valley fills with color. PA DCNR 2025 reports: Laurel and Chestnut Ridges peak in early October at elevation, lower Fayette/Westmoreland up to two weeks behind.
pois.confidence_note: Seasonal intel from secondary sources; verify actual dates before planning.

pois.name: Fall foliage: Late peak (late October) — Pittsburgh area trails
pois.locator: Montour Trail, Three Rivers Heritage Trail, Pittsburgh metro, Panhandle Trail PA section
pois.desc: Pittsburgh metro (Montour, Three Rivers, Panhandle east end) peaks around Oct 21–30 most years. Oaks color latest and in shades of russet, brown, and deep red — still beautiful. American beech holds golden through mid-November.
pois.tip: The Montour's Greer Tunnel / Peters Township section is particularly colorful in late October — the high bridge over Chartiers Creek with fall color is a great photo stop.
pois.confidence_note: Seasonal intel from secondary sources; verify actual dates before planning.

pois.name: Trout fishing season calendar — PA & MD
pois.locator: Youghiogheny River (Confluence to Ohiopyle), Casselman River (Rockwood to Confluence), Laurel Hill Creek, Flaugherty Creek (near Meyersdale)
pois.desc: PA statewide Opening Day: first Saturday of April (2026: April 4; 2025: April 5). Pre-season stocking starts mid-February — no fishing on Stocked Trout Waters from stocking date to Opening Day 8am. Mentored Youth Day: last Saturday of March. Fall/winter stockings Oct 1–mid December on 118 popular sections. Extended Trout Season Sept 2–Feb 16 with 3-fish/day limit. Yough trophy section (Ramcat Run to Rt. 381 bridge, ~9 mi): all-tackle trophy trout, PA license + trout permit required, closed April 1 to season opener. MD: Yough catch-and-release area maintains ~1,300 trout/mile with both brown and rainbow.
pois.tip: Fall is prime for large brown trout on the Yough — spawning run brings up fish. The Yough stays cool year-round due to dam releases at Confluence, so summer fishing is viable unlike many PA streams. Check pfbc.pa.gov for county-specific stocking schedules.
pois.confidence_note: Seasonal intel from secondary sources; verify actual dates before planning.

pois.name: Hunting season on and near the Rachel Carson Trail
pois.locator: Rachel Carson Trail corridor (private land) and adjacent public lands
pois.desc: The RCT crosses much private land where hunting is legal. Key dates to be aware of for trail safety: Archery deer season (Oct 3–Nov 20, late Dec–Jan). Rifle deer season (Saturday after Thanksgiving, ~2 weeks — this is the high-risk period). Muzzleloader (Oct 17–25). Grouse and small game open Oct–Nov. Blaze orange (250 sq in) required for everyone in the woods during firearms deer seasons in PA — even hikers and trail runners. The RCT Conservancy notes that trail use continues year-round and reminds users to wear orange during gun season.
pois.tip: Wear blaze orange during late November to mid-December rifle season if hiking the RCT or GAP's Pennsylvania sections near game land. The Saturday after Thanksgiving is Opening Day of rifle season — avoid the RCT this weekend if possible as hunter activity is at its peak.
pois.confidence_note: Seasonal intel from secondary sources; verify actual dates before planning.

pois.name: Hunting seasons — general trail awareness
pois.locator: Wooded sections of all trails, especially Rachel Carson, GAP near Forbes State Forest, Panhandle WV section
pois.desc: Key seasons in SW PA and MD: Archery deer (Oct 3–Nov 20 statewide; late Dec–Jan). Rifle deer (Saturday after Thanksgiving for ~2 weeks). Wild turkey fall season (Nov 1–14). Bear seasons (Oct–Nov). Grouse/small game (Oct–Feb). All Sunday hunting now legal in PA year-round following 2025 law change. 250 sq inches blaze orange required during firearms seasons. Most paved rail-trails (Montour, Three Rivers, GAP) are not within hunting areas, but the Rachel Carson Trail and some GAP sections near Forbes State Forest pass through active hunting territory.
pois.tip: If you look like a deer, dress like a hunter. Bright colors or blaze orange any time you're in the woods during archery season (Oct–Nov). During rifle season: mandatory 250 sq inches blaze orange on head, chest, and back.
pois.confidence_note: Seasonal intel from secondary sources; verify actual dates before planning.

pois.name: Big Savage Tunnel seasonal closure (Dec–early April)
pois.locator: Big Savage Tunnel, ~Mile 22 of GAP (MD/PA border area)
pois.desc: The Big Savage Tunnel (3,291 ft, the longest on the GAP) closes approximately December through early April each year to protect the bat population that overwinters inside. Through-riders must plan around this closure. Check gaptrail.org/trail-alerts for the exact dates each season.
pois.tip: This closure is non-negotiable — the tunnel is gated. Through-riders doing Pittsburgh-to-Cumberland in winter must take a road bypass. Check gaptrail.org before planning a winter ride.
pois.confidence_note: Seasonal intel from official trail authority; dates may shift slightly year to year.

pois.name: National Tunnel closure (verify status)
pois.desc: The National Tunnel (630 ft, Cecil Township, Mile 25) was closed in November 2025 after engineers found significant concrete ceiling deterioration at the western entrance. Full rehabilitation requires an estimated $2.75 million — the largest single repair in MTC history. Construction cannot begin until funding is secured; completion is not expected before 2027. An active crowdfunding campaign with dollar-for-dollar match (until July 1, 2026) is running at nationaltunnel.org. Alternate access while closed: McConnell Road trailhead (Mile 24.7) heading west, Kurnick trailhead (Mile 25.6) heading east. Do NOT attempt an on-road bypass — MTC advises against it.
pois.tip: Confirmed closed November 2025 per TrailLink and Observer-Reporter. This is NOT the same as the Greer Tunnel (which was repaired and reopened May 2024).
pois.correction_note: MAJOR UPDATE: The National Tunnel's expected reopening was initially stated as 'unknown'. As of May 2026, the MTC needs $2.75 million for full rehabilitation. Construction cannot begin until 2027 at the earliest. Active fundraising campaign with dollar-for-dollar match until July 1, 2026 — see nationaltunnel.org. Alternate access: McConnell Road trailhead (Mile 24.7) heading west; Kurnick trailhead (Mile 25.6) heading east.
pois.confidence_note: Confirmed: closed Nov 2025; $2.75M repair; 2027 timeline per Observer-Reporter May 2026 and nationaltunnel.org.

pois.name: Tandem Connection — hours & services
pois.locator: Mile 27.1, Georgetown Rd & Morganza Rd, Hendersonville/Canonsburg (0.4 mi from Southpointe exit I-79)
pois.desc: Open year-round with seasonally adjusted hours — call ahead or check tandemconnection.com/visitus. Services: bike sales (Fuji, Giant, Bianchi, Cervelo, SE BMX), rentals (hybrids, tandems, fat-tire winter bikes, e-bikes), repairs, storage, bike accessories. Food: Perry's Premium Ice Cream (up to 32 flavors), BBQ sandwiches (Roadside Ribs), coffee and espresso, snacks. Cross-country ski rentals in winter. Indoor/outdoor seating. Parking. Phone: 724-745-2453.
pois.tip: Call or check website before arriving — seasonal hours vary and the shop has been known to have irregular holiday schedules. Best to confirm for early-season or late-fall visits.
pois.confidence_note: Business confirmed open; phone number (724-745-2453) consistent across sources.

pois.name: Sager Mosaics & Ruins Project — tour booking required
pois.locator: Mile 104–105, GAP, Whitsett (Perryopolis area), Fayette County
pois.desc: Ruins Project guided tours: mid-April through Thanksgiving, selected weekend days only, reservation required via sagermosaics.com. Tours are ~90 minutes outdoors. $30/person. CLOSED Tuesdays and Wednesdays. Studio (jewelry, art, gifts, ice cream sandwiches) is walk-in during open hours. Tours fill on peak summer weekends — book weeks ahead.
pois.tip: The tour genuinely requires advance booking. Walk-ins to the studio are always welcome. Mid-April open date means you cannot visit in winter.
pois.correction_note: Tour price confirmed $30/person. Closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays. Ruins Project tours run selected weekend days mid-April through Thanksgiving. Reservation URL updated.
